# omada_firewall_acl_order (Resource)

Manages the ordering of firewall ACL rules on the Omada Controller. Omada assigns rule index (first-match order) by creation order; this resource owns the global order for a site+type pair by issuing a batch modifyIndex command. Delete is a no-op — removing this resource stops managing order without altering rules.

Firewall ACL rules are first-match; the order in which rules are evaluated determines which rule applies to a given packet. By default, Omada assigns index based on creation order. This resource lets you declaratively set the order of a managed set of rule IDs, overriding creation order.

~> **Important:** This resource only manages order for the rule IDs you provide in `rule_ids`. Rules not listed are left untouched. To add or modify rules, use the `omada_firewall_acl` resource; to set their order, use this resource.

<!-- schema generated by tfplugindocs -->
## Schema

### Required

- `rule_ids` (List of String) Ordered list of ACL rule IDs. Position in the list sets the first-match index (1-based). On Read the list reflects the controller's current ordering so drift surfaces as a plan diff.
- `site_id` (String) The ID of the site this resource belongs to.

### Optional

- `type` (Number) ACL type: 0=gateway (default), 1=switch, 2=eap. Changing the type replaces the resource — a different ACL type is a distinct ordered set.

### Read-Only

- `id` (String) Resource identifier in the form '{site_id}:{type}'.

## Example Usage

```terraform
# Create some firewall ACL rules
resource "omada_firewall_acl" "rule_1" {
  site_id          = omada_site.example.id
  name             = "Allow HTTPS from Guest"
  type             = 0  # gateway
  policy           = 1  # permit
  protocols        = [6]  # TCP
  source_type      = 0  # network
  source_ids       = [omada_network.guest.id]
  destination_type = 0  # network
  destination_ids  = [omada_network.trusted.id]
}

resource "omada_firewall_acl" "rule_2" {
  site_id          = omada_site.example.id
  name             = "Deny all Guest to Trusted"
  type             = 0  # gateway
  policy           = 0  # deny
  protocols        = [6, 17]  # TCP, UDP
  source_type      = 0  # network
  source_ids       = [omada_network.guest.id]
  destination_type = 0  # network
  destination_ids  = [omada_network.trusted.id]
}

# Declaratively set the order: rule_1 is evaluated first (index 1),
# rule_2 is evaluated second (index 2)
resource "omada_firewall_acl_order" "gateway" {
  site_id  = omada_site.example.id
  type     = 0  # gateway
  rule_ids = [
    omada_firewall_acl.rule_1.id,
    omada_firewall_acl.rule_2.id,
  ]
}
```

## Import

Import an ACL order by `{site_id}:{type}`:

```shell
terraform import omada_firewall_acl_order.gateway 'siteId:0'
```

On import, all rules of the given type are adopted as the managed set and their current controller order is read into `rule_ids`. You can then prune the list to manage only a subset of rules.
